pages.text: Performance of self-compacting concrete containing different mineral admixtures Mucteba Uysal ⇑, Mansur Sumer Sakarya University, Civil Engineering Department, Sakarya 54187, Turkey article info Article history: Received 16 December 2010 Received in revised form 23 March 2011 Accepted 14 April 2011 Available online 31 May 2011 Keywords: Self-compacting concrete Workability Compressive strength Sulphate resistance abstract This paper presents experimental study on the properties of self-compacting concrete (SCC). Portland cement (PC) was replaced with ﬂy ash (FA), granulated blast furnace slag (GBFS), limestone powder (LP), basalt powder (BP) and marble powder (MP) in various proportioning rates. The inﬂuence of mineral admixtures on the workability, compressive strength, ultrasonic pulse velocity, density and sulphate resistance of SCC was investigated. Sulphate resistance tests involved immersion in 10% magnesium sul- phate and 10% sodium sulphate solutions for a period of 400 days. The degree of sulphate attack was eval- uated using visual examination and reduction in compressive strength. The test results showed that among the mineral admixtures used, FA and GBFS signiﬁcantly increased the workability and compres- sive strength of SCC mixtures. Replacing 25% of PC with FA resulted in a strength of more than 105 MPa at 400 days. Moreover, the presence of mineral admixtures had a beneﬁcial effect on the strength loss due to sodium and magnesium sulphate attack. On the other hand, the best resistance to sodium and magne- sium sulphate attacks was obtained from a combination of 40% GBFS with 60% PC. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Self-compacting concrete (SCC) was ﬁrst developed in Japan in the late 1980s as a material that can ﬂow through congested rein- forcing bars without need for additional consolidation and without undergoing any signiﬁcant separation under its own weight [1–3]. SCC increases productivity levels leading to shortened concrete construction time, makes the construction of heavily congested structural elements and hard to reach areas easier, improves in situ concrete quality in difﬁcult casting loading reduces noise- and vibration-related injuries, and helps in achieving higher surface quality [4]. One of the disadvantages of SCC is its cost, associated with the use of high volumes of Portland cement and use of chem- ical admixtures. One alternative to reduce the cost of SCC is the use of mineral admixtures such as LP and FA, which are ﬁnely divided materials added to concrete during mixture procedure [5]. When these mineral admixtures replace part of the PC, the cost of SCC will be reduced especially if the mineral admixtures are waste or indus- trial by-product. Moreover, the use of mineral admixtures in the production of SCC not only provides economical beneﬁts but also reduces heat of hydration [6]. The incorporation of mineral admix- tures also eliminates the need for viscosity-enhancing chemical admixtures. The lower water content of the concrete leads to higher durability, in addition to better mechanical integrity of the struc- ture. It is also known that some mineral admixtures may improve rheological properties and reduce thermally-induced cracking of concrete due to the reduction in the overall heat of hydration and increase the workability and long-term properties of concrete [7,8]. FA, GBFS, silica fume, LP were the most frequently carried out in SCC [9–15]. But, BP as industr

Since the last decade, SCC has been widely used in areas vulnerable to sulphate attack such as substructures, infrastructure and indus- trial ﬂoors. Sulphate attack has often been discussed in terms of the reaction between the hydrates in cement pastes and dissolved compounds, such as sodium sulphate or magnesium sulphate, in the attacking solution [26]. The classical form of sulphate attack in- volves alkali sulphates such as sodium sulphate which reacts with calcium hydroxide and calcium aluminate hydrate to form gypsum and ettringite, The gypsum and ettringite formed as a result of sul- phate attack is signiﬁcantly more voluminous (1.2–2.2 times) than the initial reactants, which can cause expansion, cracking, and deterioration of concrete by the reaction of SO 2/C0 4 ions [27]. A mag- nesium sulphate solution may cause the deterioration of concrete due to the formation of Mg-containing hydrates (e.g. M–S–H gel), as well as gypsum and thaumasite. Some researchers have re- ported on the sulphate resistance imparted by mineral admixtures, which is generally incorporated in concrete to improve its engi- neering properties and durability [28]. The objective of this study is to investigate the inﬂuence of min- eral admixtures on the fresh, hardened and sulphate resistance properties of SCC. Performance of mineral admixtures is estab- lished in SCC by fresh concrete tests such as slump ﬂow, T 50 time, V-funnel time and L-box ratio, and hardened concrete tests such as compressive strength, ultrasonic pulse velocity and density. More- over, the resistance against sulphate attacks, in particular, sodium and magnesium sulphate environments, of SCC mixtures contain- ing mineral admixtures exposed to different solution concentra- tions. Experimental results of sulphate resistance include reduction in compressive strength loss and visual examination which have been subjected to 10% sodium and 10% magnesium sulphate solutions for 400 days. 2. Experimental procedure 2.1. Materials The Portland cement used in this study complied with EN 197-1 and labelled as CEM I 42.5N. Speciﬁc surface area by Blaine and 28th day compressive strength of cement were 399.6 m 2/kg, and 48.3 MPa, respectively. A natural river sand and crushed limestone with a maximum size of 16 mm was used as ﬁne and coarse aggre- gates, respectively. The speciﬁc gravity and water absorption prop- erties of river sand and crushed limestone are 2.59%, 1.44%, and 2.73%, 0.22%, respectively. Besides, GBFS, FA, MP, LP and BP were used in SCC mixtures in order to establish the performance of min- eral admixtures. GBFS was provided by Akcansa cement grinding plant. FA procured from Cayirhan power plant and can be classiﬁed as Class C according to ASTM C 618. MP was obtained from a mar- ble managing plant in Bilecik directly used in SCC without any pro- cesses. BP and LP were by-product of quarry crushers and collected from the ﬁltration system of a quarry crushers. The particle size distributions of these materials were obtained by a laser scattering technique and are presented in Fig. 1 . The physical, chemical and mechanical characteristics of Portland cement and mineral admix- tures used in this study are given in Table 1 . 2.2. Mixture proportions Sixteen series of mix proportions, of which one control and 15 mixtures with mineral admixtures were prepared in different com- binations. The proportions of the produced mixtures are given in Table 2 . As seen in that table, the mixtures were

of 1.6% of binder materials by weight for reducing the water/binder ratio of SCC. 2.3. Casting, testing and curing of specimens Compressive strength, density, ultrasonic pulse velocity and sulphate resistance measurements were made on 100 mm cube SCC specimens. All specimens, with or without mineral admix- tures, were demoulded in a controlled chamber at 20 ± 2 /C176C after 1 day of casting, and cured in water at 20 /C176C and 60% RH. Speci- mens were then cast in steel moulds and were not subjected to any compaction other than their own self-weights. In the fresh state, slump ﬂow diameter, V-funnel time, L-box ratio and T50 times of the SCC mixtures were measured according to the EFNARC Committee’s suggestions [29]. In hardened concrete testing, com- pressive strength and UPV were performed at 7, 28, 90 and 400 days except density test, it was performed at 28 days. The sul- phate exposure testing procedure was conducted by immersing concrete specimens after the speciﬁed initial curing in a water tank containing 10% sodium sulphate and 10% magnesium sulphate solutions at 23 ± 2 /C176C according to ASTM C1012-04 standard [30]. Sulphate solutions used for the immersion tests were renewed every 4 weeks to minimize the increase in pH due to the leaching of OH/C0 ions from the concrete specimens. Sulphate concentrations were kept relatively high to accelerate deterioration and also to compensate for small variations in concentration during the tests. One-third of the specimens were placed in a solution of 10% so- dium sulphate, one-third in a solution of 10% magnesium sulphate, and the rest (one-third) of the specimens placed in tap water. A stirrer was placed in the basin in order to secure a good circulation of the solution around the specimens. The deterioration of the SCC cube specimens was investigated by determining the compressive strength loss, which was calcu- lated as follows: Compressive strength loss ð%Þ¼½ ð A /C0 BÞ=A/C138/C2 100 where A is the average compressive strength of SCC specimens cured in tap water (MPa) and B is the average compressive strength of SCC specimens immersed in test solutions (MPa) for the same period. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Properties of fresh concrete The results of fresh properties of all SCC mixtures are included in Figs. 1 and 2. The Fig. 1 shows the properties such as slump-ﬂow and T50 time of SCC mixtures. The slump ﬂow indicates the mean Table 1 Properties of Portland cement and mineral admixtures. Materials (kg/m 3) Cement FA GBFS BP MP LP Water w/p w/c Sand CSI CSII Control 550 – – – – – 182 0.33 0.33 869 467 311 FA15 467 83 – – – – 182 0.33 0.39 865 457 305 FA25 412 138 – – – – 182 0.33 0.44 887 451 301 FA35 357 193 – – – – 182 0.33 0.51 878 445 297 GBFS20 440 – 110 – – – 182 0.33 0.41 866 465 310 GBFS40 330 – 220 – – – 182 0.33 0.55 863 463 309 GBFS60 220 – 3

diameter of the mass of concrete after release of a standard slump cone; the diameter is measured in two perpendicular directions. In terms of slump ﬂow, all SCC mixtures exhibited satisfactory slump ﬂows in the range of 690–750 mm, which is an indication of a good deformability. FA and GBFS series have shown higher and LP, BP, MP series showed lower slump-ﬂow values. Due to its spherical shape, FA can disperse agglomeration of cement particles. When cement is replaced by FA, a lower dosage of superplasticiser is re- quired to maintain the same ﬁlling ability. Because superplasticizer dosage kept constant for all the mixtures, FA series had more superplasticizer dosage to provide same slump ﬂow than other mixtures and have shown higher slump-ﬂow values. Moreover, comparing to the other mineral admixtures, the FA particles had a spherical geometry and a coarse particle size, causing a reduction in the surface area. In addition, a partial replacement of cement by FA results in higher volume of paste due to its lower density and this increase in the paste volume reduces the friction at the ﬁne aggregate-paste interface and improves the cohesiveness and plas- ticity, and thus leads to increased workability [31]. The slump ﬂow time for the concrete to reach diameter of 50 cm ( T50) for all the mixtures was less than 5 s and all SCC mixtures showed ﬂow time values in the range of 2–5 s. Both the slump-ﬂow values and the T50 times are in good agreement to that of the values given by Euro- pean guidelines for range of applications [29]. In addition to the slump ﬂow and T50 time tests, V-funnel test was also performed to assess the ﬂowability and stability of the SCC. V-funnel time is the elapsed time in seconds between the open- ing of the bottom outlet depending upon the time after which opened and the time when the light becomes visible from the bot- tom, when observed from the top. The V-funnel ﬂow times were in the range of 9–18 s. It was suggested by EFNARC Committee that 11 and 15 s for lower and upper limits of V-funnel time, respec- tively, are acceptable for designing the appropriate SCC mixtures. In Fig. 3, test results of this investigation indicated that all SCC mix- tures do not meet the requirements of allowable ﬂow time. There- fore, V-funnel time above 15 s would be very cohesive and hard to handle. L-box test (time taken to reach 400 mm distance (T400), time taken to reach 800 mm distance (T800) and the ratio of heights at the two edges of L-box) represents the ﬁlling and passing ability of SCC. L-box test is more sensitive to blocking. There is a risk of blocking of the mixture when the L-box blocking ratio is below 0.8 [29]. Block- ing ratio of SCC produced with mineral admixtures is also given in Fig. 3 . Blocking ratio ( h2/h1) must be between 0.8 and 1.00. Maxi- mum size of coarse aggregate was kept as 16 mm in order to avoid blocking effect in the L-box test and all the mixtures of SCC have re- mained in target range which is as per EFNARC standards. 3.2. Properties of hardened concrete The hardened properties that were stated for all the mixtures were the compressive strength and ultrasonic pulse velocity at 7, 28, 90 and 400 days and the density at 28 days. Table 3 presents the mean of compressive strength as determined from three speci- mens. As seen in that table, control mixture at 28 days was accepted as reference mixture and it is aimed to compare compressive strength values of other mixtures at different ages by p

nucleation sites for the early reaction products of CH and CSH, which will accelerate the hydration of cement clinkers (especially C3S) and consequently increase the early age compressive strength [33]. Besides, one of the best ways to explain the increase in strength of MP series is heterogeneous nucleation, which has al- ready been investigated by several authors, is probably the main physical effect in charge of the increase in strength associated with the ﬁneness of the MP series used [34]. Heterogeneous nucleation is a physical process leading to a chemical activation of the hydra- tion of cement. It is related to the nucleation of hydrates on foreign mineral particles, which catalyzes the nucleation process by reduc- ing the energy barrier. It depends on: the amount of mineral admixture used, since the probability for nucleation sites to be near to cement particles increases with the amount of foreign par- ticles, the ﬁneness of mineral admixture particles, since the de- crease of particle size favours nucleation and the afﬁnity of the mineral powder to cement hydrates, which is related to the nature of the mineral used. However, MP is the most ﬁneness material in all SCC mixtures. So, it governs to chemical activation of the hydration of cement. Moreover, MP is not pozzolan, but nor fully inert as it reacts with the alumina phases of the cement. If the ce- ment has a remarkable amount of tricalcium aluminate (C 3A), cal- cium carboaluminate will be produced from the reaction between calcium carbonate (CaCO 3) from the MP and the C 3A [35]. This reaction, accelerating the hydration and increasing the compres- sive strength, increases with the C 3A content of the cement and speciﬁc surface area and the ﬁneness of the ﬁller. However, MP caused an increasing of early age performance of SCC. The addition of FA and GBFS to SCC mixtures had positively af- fected late age behaviour at 90 and 400 days. However, FA and GBFS series have shown the best performance due to the conver- sion of portlandite into secondary C–S–H gel, through strong poz- zolanic reactions. The compressive strength values were in the range of 89.08–72.42 MPa at 90 days and were in the range of 105.69–78.38 MPa, respectively at 400 days. For all SCC series the compressive strength decreased with the increasing mineral admixtures content at all ages. Table 4 presents the UPV values for all mixtures at different ages. The UPV value is related to the density of constituent materials and can be used to assess strength of concrete for a given aggregate and a given moisture condition. The trend in UPV is similar to that of compressive strength in this experiment. Khatib also reported a close relation between UPV and compressive strength [15]. Ultra- sonic velocity has increased by increasing compressive strength for all the mixtures. The correlation between the compressive strength and UPV is quite strong ( R = 0.85 for all the mixtures). Table 4 also presents the density values for all SCC mixtures at 28 days and each density values represents the average density. Generally, GBFS series have shown the highest density values. This can be attributed to the better compaction and reduction in voids in concrete containing GBFS. At the same mineral admixture series, the incorporation of increasing amounts of mineral admixtures in SCC mixtures causes a systematic reduction in density. 3.3. Resistance to sulphate attack of concrete 3.3.1. Visual examination A thorough visual insp

typical examples of damage of SCC specimens subjected to sulphate attack after 400 days of immersion in both sulphate solu- tions. Although it was observed that a small amount of spalling at edges and corners of the GBFS40 specimen which showed the best performance exposed to 10% magnesium sulphate solution for 400 days. Different results of visual examination were observed on the control specimen. In addition white substance was depos- ited on the face of the control specimen it was affected the worst in this solution exhibited relatively severe spalling at edges and corners. After 400 days of immersion in sodium sulphate solution, the visual examination of the GBFS40 specimen showed no visible deterioration with no detectable weight loss, as shown in Fig. 5 . Furthermore, the MP10 specimen exhibited visible deterioration spalling concrete especially at the corners and edges and some cracking. The visual examination of SCC specimens stored in the magnesium and sodium sulphate solutions for 400 days, Fig. 4 and 5 indicated that the intensity of the damage by magnesium sulphate attack was greater than sodium sulphate attack. 3.3.2. Compressive strength loss As mentioned earlier, 100 mm cubic specimens were prepared following the guide lines of ASTM C 39 to examine the loss of strength of all the test specimens [36]. The evolution of the strength loss of SCC specimens placed in sodium sulphate and magnesium sulphate solutions is shown in Figs. 6 and 7 , respec- tively. As can be seen from Figs. 6 and 7 , these strength results are discussed in terms of strength loss in order to highlight the damage caused to SCC mixtures by exposure to sulphate attack. After 400 days exposure in the MgSO 4 solution, the amounts of strength loss have been measured in the range of 4.55–13.09% for SCC mixtures ( Fig. 6 ). The strength loss decreased as the replacement of mineral admixtures increased; For example, the strength loss of FA35, was about 6.91%, compared to strength loss of 10.34% for the FA15 specimen. It is clear that the mixtures con- taining GBFS exhibited a much better resistance to magnesium sul- phate attack than the mixtures containing other mineral admixtures. Moreover, these results indicate that the incorporation of mineral admixtures leads to reduced strength loss compared with those having only PC (control). The overall message from the data shown in Fig. 7 is that the best resistance to sodium sulphate attack was obtained with GBFS ser- ies. However, FA series performed very well resistance to sodium sulphate attack and showed lower strength loss than other series. The amounts of strength loss have been measured in the order of 1.52–9.68% for SCC mixtures, after 400 days exposure in the NaSO 4 solution. A nearly similar trend was observed in sodium sulphate solution and test results showed that the incorporation of mineral Fig. 4. (a) The best case (GBFS40 specimen) and (b) the worst case (control specimen) of specimens in 10% magnesium sulphate solution after 400 days exposure. Fig. 5. (a) The best case (GBFS40 specimen) and (b) the worst case (MP10 specimen) of specimens in 10% sodium sulphate solution after 400 days exposure. Table 4 Ultrasound pulse velocity and density of SCC mixtures. Ultrasound pulse velocity (m/s) Density (kg/m 3) Code 7 days 28 days 90 days 400 days 28 days Control 4416 4650 4796 4995 2.43 FA15 4375 4621 4750 4992 2.41 FA25 4286 4593 4808 5014 2.39 FA35 4210 4535 4702 4935 2.38 GBFS20 4292 4680 4823 4989 2.4

admixtures led to reduced strength loss compared with those hav- ing only PC (control) except MP10 specimen. However, the overall strength loss in sodium sulphate solution is much less pronounced than that in magnesium sulphate solution. It is evident from these data that the use of SCC without the addition of mineral admixtures in both environments might result in severe deterioration in terms of strength loss. Based on the strength results, it is concluded that mineral admixtures, such as LP, BP, MP and especially GBFS and FA improve the resistance of SCCs against sulphate attack. Sulphate attack on concrete is characterized by the chemical reaction of sulphate ions as the aggressive substance and the aluminate component of hardened cement paste [37]. The reaction between these substances, if enough water is present, produces ettringite and gypsum and causes expansion of the concrete, leading to cracking. At the same time as the sulphate attack, the attack of magnesium ions and, to a lesser extent, the sodium ions on C–S–H starts when CH is depleted. This attack leads to gypsum precipitation and decalciﬁcation of C–S–H. The decalciﬁcation of C–S–H destroys the binding capacity of C–S–H and leads to a loss of strength in concrete [38]. According to test results, control specimen had the highest strength loss among all the series in magnesium sulphate environ- ments. The main reason of strength loss of control specimen that ettringite and gypsum formation caused spalling and cracking, Fig. 7. Compressive strength loss of SCC mixtures subjected to sulphate attack in 10% sodium sulphate solution. Fig. 6. Compressive strength loss of SCC mixtures subjected to sulphate attack in 10% magnesium sulphate solution. 4118 M. Uysal, M. Sumer / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 4112–4120

while destruction of C–S–H caused softening and disintegration. However, ettringite formation was the dominant effect in control specimen exposed to sulphate attack in magnesium sulphate envi- ronment. This observation is in agreement with the ﬁndings of some researchers who concluded that ettringite formation was the main mechanism of deterioration in the mixture which had not mineral admixtures (plain concrete). The ettringite formation is low with blends high in GBFS because it is rich in the aluminium that is not in the form available for reaction [39]. So, GBFS series showed the best performance with in all the series in that solution. Moreover, the superior resistance of the mixtures containing GBFS against sulphate attack is attributed to the pore reﬁnement process occurring due to the conversion of lime forming from the hydration of cement into additional binding material through lime pozzolan reaction. Furthermore, these pozzolanic materials play an impor- tant role in improving the aggregate-paste bond through the den- siﬁcation of the transition zone and formation of more calcium silicate hydrates [40]. In agreement with previous investigations, visual examination of concrete cubes exposed to sodium and magnesium sulphate solutions and their compressive strength reduction showed that attack by magnesium sulphate was more aggressive in its action on SCC mixtures than sodium sulphate attack. The main reason is that the conversion of secondary C–S–H gel into M–S–H (or M–C–S–H) gel, which is a noncementitious product and results in the softening of cement matrix, eventually led to the pronounced more deterioration on SCC specimens exposed to magnesium sul- phate environment than sodium sulphate environment [41]. Moon et al. reported in literature conﬁrm this case that the deterioration in the case of magnesium sulphate attack to be more severe than in the case of sodium sulphate attack [42]. However, GBFS and FA ser- ies performed the highest performance in the sodium sulphate solution. The increase in the sulphate resistance of GBFS and FA series with increasing the GBFS and FA replacement level is ex- plained by the following mechanisms. First, the replacement of a portion of Portland cement with GBFS and FA reduces the total amount of tricalcium aluminate hydrate in the cement paste ma- trix of concrete. The second mechanism is through the pozzolanic reaction between the calcium hydroxide and pozzolanic materials released during the hydration of cement, which consumes part of the calcium hydroxides. Thus, the quantity of expansive gypsum formed by the reaction of calcium hydroxide will be less in GBFS and FA series than in control specimen. Furthermore, the formation of secondary C–S–H by the pozzolanic reaction, although less dense than the primary C–S–H gel, is effective in ﬁlling and seg- menting large capillary pores into small, discontinuous capillary pores through pore size reﬁnement. Thus, the total permeability of concrete decreases. LP, BP and MP have the ﬁller action due to the ﬁner particle size of LP, BP and MP further densiﬁes the pore structure to enhance the resistance to sulphate attack [38]. 4. Conclusions Laboratory tests were performed to determine some fresh, hardened and sulphate resistance properties of SCC mixtures and the results of the tests are as follows: – Among the mineral admixtures considered, the best perfor- mance has been obtained for FA series as workability proper- ties. In general, the use
